title: Post-Selection Free Generation of Multi-Photon Added Coherent States
authors: Mariano Uria, Ricardo Gutiérrez-Jáuregui, Carla Hermann-Avigliano, Pablo Solano
abstract: Non-Gaussian quantum states are essential resources for continuous-variable quantum information processing and for metrology. Among these, multi-photon added coherent states bridge classical and non-classical behaviors; however, their generation typically relies on small photon numbers and probabilistic heralding schemes. Here, we propose a protocol for the post-selection free generation of high fidelity multi-photon added coherent states using the photon blockade effect in a driven Kerr nonlinear resonator, where such states emerge naturally during the dynamics. We demonstrate that high-fidelity states can be prepared by optimizing the external drive power and the interaction time. Furthermore, we show that the protocol is robust under realistic experimental conditions, achieving fidelities of $\approx 99\%$ with current state-of-the-art parameters. Our results unlock a deterministic route to complex non-classical states using well-established quantum optical platforms.
summary: This paper presents a deterministic method to generate multi-photon added coherent states (PACS) using a driven Kerr nonlinear resonator. By leveraging the photon blockade effect, the protocol avoids probabilistic measurement steps, achieving high fidelity even when considering realistic cavity dissipation. This provides a robust platform for creating complex non-classical resources for quantum technologies.
main_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of deterministically generating high-fidelity multi-photon added coherent states (PACS) without relying on probabilistic post-selection schemes.
main_result: A protocol is proposed and demonstrated that achieves fidelities exceeding 99% for generating PACS using a driven Kerr nonlinear resonator, even under realistic dissipative conditions.
method: The generation relies on exploiting the photon blockade effect within the cavity dynamics, monitored using a frame-independent witness called the remnant photon number ($n_r$).
model_or_system: The system is a single-mode cavity containing a Kerr nonlinear medium, modeled by a Hamiltonian $H_R$ in the rotating frame. The dynamics are analyzed using both unitary evolution and the Lindblad master equation to account for dissipation.
key_observables: Remnant Photon Number ($n_r$), Fidelity ($F(t)$), Mean Photon Number ($\langle\hat{n}(t)angle$), and the time evolution of the state in phase space.
important_parameters: The Kerr nonlinearity ($\chi$), cavity detuning ($\delta$), external drive power ($E$), and cavity decay rate ($\kappa$). The condition $\delta = 2\chi$ is noted as central for certain analyses.
main_assumptions: The initial state is assumed to be the vacuum state. The analysis involves heuristic approximations and the assumption that $n_r$ is a superior, displacement-independent witness for non-classicality.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the protocol schematic, the photon number distribution showing blockade effects, and quantitative results showing fidelity vs. time/parameters for PACS generation.
paper_structure: The paper develops the theoretical framework by defining the Hamiltonian and the remnant photon number witness. It then details the protocol, showing how the system dynamics naturally lead to the desired state, followed by numerical and analytical confirmation of high fidelity.
why_interesting: This work directly applies advanced concepts from cavity QED and nonlinear quantum optics (Kerr media, photon blockade) to create a deterministic source of complex non-classical states, which is a major goal in quantum information science.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Schematic of the protocol for high fidelity gener- ation of PACS. A coherent drive of amplitude E excites a single-mode cavity with Kerr nonlinearity χ and decay rate κ. Starting from the vacuum state |vac⟩, the system reaches a state resembling a displaced multi-photon added coherent state ˆa†n |α⟩at a time t. The lower panel illustrates the trans- formation in phase space, highlighting the emergence of non- classical features from an initially Gaussian state.

FIG. 2. Photon number distribution as a function of evolution time for different driving values (E/χ = 1.5, 4.5, 7.25, 10, 13, 16) and fixed δ = 2χ. The roman nu- merals represent the number of added photons for the best PACS generated in each driving regime. The insets on the top represent the Wigner function of the best PACS found in the left (n = 1) and right (n = 6) cases.

FIG. 3. Fidelities and optimal parameters for PACS gen- eration. The horizontal axis represents the drive amplitude E normalized by the nonlinear parameter χ. From top to bottom: Wigner function representations, optimal evolution time, amplitude of the coherent component, and optimal fi- delity of the obtained PACS. The shapes connected by colored curves represent the different number of photons added n in the obtained PACS. We considered a drive detuning δ = 2χ for all the calculations.

FIG. 4. Infidelity for the optimal cases under decoherence for different driving amplitudes E, with optimized numbers of added photons n. The vertical dashed gray line indicates the experimental state-of-the-art relation between the deco- herence rate κ and the nonlinearity χ, as reported in Ref. [21].
